Transcript:
So, we found out that my wife and you
grew up in the same place.
>> Yep.
>> Elmhurst, Queens.
>> Queens, baby.
>> Represent. Let's go.
Um
one thing we also have in common, too,
is we both started our sales careers
going door-to-door.
>> Yep.
>> So, I I started my sales career
it was my first adult job, I say, 18
years old going door-to-door like
selling house painting services.
And uh I had braces at the time, didn't
know anything about sales, definitely
had never painted a house before.
And the first thing I did was go to our
Borders, back when they had Borders, and
I bought Jeffrey Gitomer's Little Red
Book of Selling. And then I bought a
House Painting for Dummies book.
>> [laughter]
>> How did How was that? So, that's one
thing, knowing what to do. Like, how How
did you have the courage to go knock on
the door? Like, how long did it take you
to actually knock on your first
>> Dude, if I'm being honest, I didn't know
that I would be knocking on doors when I
signed up for that. And this was a
They called it an internship, but it was
like uh
100% commission-based sales job.
>> Um so, when I did find out I was going
door-to-door, I was really nervous,
dude. Cuz this is like a small town of
5,000 people. I knew that I was going to
interact with people that I knew or that
knew me of me just like from high school
sports or whatever. Um
the moment, though, that made it for me
was, and then I'll stop talking about
myself cuz this is about you today. Um
was my sales manager
uh Barry, who became my best friend. I
saw him get rejected and totally brush
it off like it was not a big deal.
>> Yep.
>> And it was like he didn't take it
personally at all. I was like, "Oh,
interesting.
>> Yep.
>> Okay, so a person can be really rude to
you and it has nothing to do with like
what you're selling or what you're
doing. We're not doing anything wrong.
You can just brush it off and just move
on to the next. That's the beauty of
sales that I did not experience in like
basketball, let's say, was if I had a
bad game, I had to wait until the next
game, which might not be till the next
week. Sales was like, I can literally
redeem myself like in 60 seconds when I
knock on the next door, you know? Well,
so, how did you get into door-to-door?
>> So, uh I went to school, grew up in
Queens, moved to New Jersey, and then
went to school college at the University
of Arizona. So, when I was there, I was
like, "Man, this place is awesome. I
want to stay out here. I want to find a
way where I can make extra money."
Um my parents were kind of trying to
convince me to come back east. My grades
weren't too doing too well, so I said,
"All right, let me figure this out. I'll
do better at school, but I'll also start
my own business. I have an idea." So,
then I called my dad up over the
weekend, and I said, "Dad, all right.
So, if you could print me out a
prototype of just like random local
stores, I'm going to go door-to-door and
sell these advertisements to these local
businesses.
>> Wow.
>> I'll mail them to dorms, fraternities,
sororities.
>> Yeah.
>> And then
um
and then that's it. So, my dad said,
"All right, if you think it's that easy
to sell, you could go sell.
I'll print, I'll mail it, I'll do all of
it for you. All you have to do is sell."
I said, "Yeah, all right, deal." So, I
went went after class one time, put on a
shirt suit and tie
in the desert. So, I'm like sweating by
the time, but I sat [laughter] in I sat
in my car looking I went to a strip
mall, and I'm looking at like these
local businesses like restaurants.
>> Yeah.
>> And I'm like, "All right, how hard can
this be?" I get in there, and like you
said, "Nah, not interested." And I was
like, "All right, that's one person."
>> And you did this by yourself though.
>> Yep. Second time, "No, not interested."
>> Were you feeling nervous though before
that or all or were you like overly
confident?
>> [laughter]
>> Definitely nervous. It took me like
sometimes it took me 2 hours just to get
out of the car to go like get a
cigarette. I either have to go back to
class or I could go into this one store
and then go back to class. So, I was
like, "Let me just go into this one
store."
>> Yeah.
>> They would say no, and then I said,
"Okay." But the worst part is like you'd
go in
and they all knew you were trying to
sell something by the way you were
dressed, and they they the vibe would
change. You're not a customer, you're
trying to make them a customer. So, I'd
always say, "Hey, is is the owner
around?" And after a while you get so
many no's and then finally one person
believes in you.
>> Yeah.
>> They give you the deal. My first deal
was uh

advertise."
>> Yeah.
>> Give me a check for like 2,500 bucks and
I was like, "Damn, this works. This
actually works." I I spent the money on
gas, materials. So, I was like, "I can't
go back now."

>> Um but that helped me because I got a

like, "I can't turn back. I already
started using I have one customer. Um

customer, that was it. There was there
was no plan B and I kept going back.

than me on the on the sales floor.

>> So that was the one thing I could

>> Yeah.
>> Yeah, I had raw talent but I wasn't
thinking the right way. Uh and then from

people. A lot of good people around you.

there's just something that
the great reps either innately or had to

who are often times 10, 20, 30 years
older than them. They obviously have way

as someone of equal status.

come from?

>> Oh, so that's experience prior.

>> I thought that was a bad bad word.
>> Oh.
>> Uh but then I realized like hey, there's
nothing wrong with being a salesperson.
>> Yeah.
>> Um
So, then once I got into the corporate
sales environment, I treated my

still kept that still kept that mindset.
And uh that's what that book
teaches you as well. Selling to Vito,
how to create that equal business
stature. Somebody You're not supposed to
put someone up on a pedestal. Even when
you're dating. Put someone up on a
pedestal, probably doesn't work out that
well.
>> Yeah.
>> Uh so, they have to respect you before
they like you and trust you. Right? So,

first when this is a business
conversation. So, let's just see if they
would appreciate what you're offering
them or or what you have to say.
Uh so, I think the best sales people,
the top sales people, they have
you know, they have that mindset. And um
I think one way we've taught our our
sales reps on how to have that mindset
is a lot of affirmations. Just
know that you could be in a meeting with
your executives with with your CEO, but
this is still your meeting as a rep.
This is still your territory. They hired
you to run this territory. So, this is
your business. This is your book of
business.
>> Mhm.
>> And you have to come across that way.
>> Dude, I love that. And
you know, like with cold calling for
example, there's so much content on
tonality and all this other stuff. And I
find that the tonality takes care of
itself when you think of yourself as a
peer.

standardization.

that? How did you navigate that?
>> So, similar to what you're doing, you
need a framework. You need to understand
the talk track,
basic plan in place.
Uh but by me sometimes getting to a
company where there were already top
reps there, and then me just observing
how they go about their day, yes, I
could see little tweaks that I could
make here or there.
But I was very conscious of saying,

different than how how I would sell."
>> Yeah.
>> So, I guess that's okay, right? I'm not
going to change this person.

>> Also not stepping in to save the day too
often. There are times where yes, the
deal is big. Okay,
let's see how far this person can take
it and then jump in because then you
have a tendency, especially coming up
the ranks as a salesperson, top sale
salesperson to manager, VP, senior VP,
you feel like I could close this deal.
Let me jump in.
>> Yeah.
>> But that's not your job anymore. Your
job is to make sure that they're
thinking about it. So I really enjoyed
uh not so much doing that anymore, but
talking to the rep before and after the
meeting just to get a sense of how
they're thinking about the meeting, how
they're thinking about the deal, their
opportunity, how they thought about
how that call went, how the meeting went
and then seeing their thought process 6
months from now.
>> Mhm.
>> How it changed.
Because I know I ask certain questions
and I have I have the urge to kind of
fill in the gaps, but what would you do
in this scenario?
And sometimes they come up with ideas
that are even more creative and I'm
like, "God, that's great." So I know
they're they're getting closer to you
know, just kind of running on their own.

point there, too, where

we really rob

happening when we try to be too rigid
with the process cuz I I am I think I
think we have a similar sales style
where I I'm extremely process driven.
But sometimes what
I forget to do is to think about like
the the politics going on the other side
or the inner dynamics between people or
creative things that I could do during a

that sometimes cuz I'm like, am I
following my process, you know?

Um

um
yeah, so there are some key There are

close the end of the

you kind of take for granted, right? So,

kind of add your own style to that. But
if you're missing the basics, those are
those are easy things to do. Customer
storytelling, I think is a great
example. Instead of telling, tell a
story.

>> And sometimes no one's ever taught a
salesperson how to tell a story the

it's it's those basic fundamentals.
That's the stuff that scales.
>> Right.
>> Right. That's the stuff that you want to
scale across the team.

you you guys have coached a ton of reps
as well.
What do you think is the missing link?
Like why why do we still work with

the what the majority of reps are doing,
and they're still not doing basic things
like setting an agenda

interesting course. I'm going to sign up
for this course." And I don't do
anything with it, right? So, you like
you have good intentions, but um
>> Yeah.
>> I don't I don't know the answer. Some
people are different. And that's why I
feel like
training is a little broken now, where
you have trainers go in, and they have
great material and information, but
they're relying on the organization and
the reps to take it upon themselves.
>> Yep.
>> Imagine if a football coach did that.
Kind of drew up some plays and say,
"I'll see you on Sunday."
>> Yeah.
>> You have to just work on the drills,
even though it has nothing to do with
the overall It's just you're focusing on
blocking and tackling and backpedaling
and just uh just little things like
that.
And then you see, "Okay, somebody's
getting better at that. Let's move on to

at that." And then eventually, you put
all these pieces together, but nobody
has the time to do that. What executive
has the time to do that?
>> Yeah. It's the
the Kirkpatrick model. Have you heard of
that?
>> No.
>> So, it's a I don't know if it's a
consulting model or how it came came
about, but it's a lot of enablement uh
leaders reference it a lot, and it's
essentially
like when something is taught, the first
level is
I forget the word that they use, but
it's essentially the sentiment.
So, it's uh we hired an outside trainer.
They came in. They did a cold calling
workshop, let's say. Let's send a survey
out to the team to see how they feel
about it.
>> Yeah.
>> Right? Um the second thing is behavior.
So, like are they actually changing
their behavior and doing like the things
that was were taught in the training?
And the third thing I'm missing a level
cuz there's four. The third thing was
like results.
Like can we tie this to like actual
results? I think the second layer was
like can they talk about it? So it was
what do they think about it, can they
talk about it and like regurgitate it
essentially, uh can they do it and does
it like generate a measurable result? I
think for the longest time until very
recently cuz there just has been so much
budget and so little scrutiny on how
it's being spent with training that it
was mostly just like how do people feel
about it?
>> Yeah.
>> Do they feel really good? Do we get
great like results in our surveys? When
we quiz them, did they get the right
answer? And there was no measurement
around like behavior change,
>> Yep.
>> the results, there was no like
reinforcement.
That's like mandatory when we work with
a client.
>> That's great.
>> Like you can't just train.
Like you have to train, you got to work
with the leaders too so they know how to
reinforce it.
>> Yep.
>> Like it's just absolutely mandatory.
>> Yeah.
>> Um
before we kind of dig into what you're
up to currently um with Simon, I'm
curious during your time as a sales
leader

team? Was Was there any kind of monthly,
quarterly or weekly kind of rhythm
around how you thought like hey,
enablement comes in and they teach this
thing um on a monthly basis and then we

kind of think about all of that cuz like
you're obviously like you have a really
strong passion around like coaching and
training and teaching and like kind of
stuff.
How did you bring that into your into
your role as a VP?
>> I mean I the ideal answer was
uh
very easily but it was so tough. I mean
the bigger the company got the tougher
became.
>> So I even it got to a point where we'd
bring all the
all the uh sales team the entire sales
team together. I think once a
I think once a month and we'd say okay,
this is the topic. We're coming up with
a new product. Here's the talk track.
I'm going to I'm going to
try the talk track. But, these are the
basic points. Make it your own. We'll
come back next month. Start practicing
with with some of your prospects. Get
real feedback. And then massage it as
you will." So, we come back the next
month.
We say, "Okay, who wants to go?"
Nobody. Nobody steps up.
>> Yeah.
>> And you realize like they didn't even
like as soon as we left the meeting,
they [laughter] totally forgot.
So, I said, "Okay, let's let's try this
again, right?" So, because this is
important. We want to make sure like
we're getting the talk track down so
when the product comes out, we're like
really good at it.
And then the the next month, like the
most junior rep answers and at least
he's saying something, but your your
senior guys, they they just they get
stuck in their ways.
And like you said, unless you're like
there reinforcing it, it's really tough,
man.
>> Yeah.
Dude, the reinforcement is such a big
thing. I
like
I think the
most overlooked part of training is the
managers. Yeah. I feel like the front

training. I know it's even worse like as
you become a director and a VP, there
isn't really like a blueprint for it.
And often time is like if you're VP or
meeting with your CRO, it's not like

most of those VPs, you know? Um but that
front line leader is so big. So,
talk to us about Simon cuz I think this
is a good segue into like
why you founded Simon.
>> Yep.
>> And like everything that it stand like
it it really exists to solve the exact
problem that we're talking about. But,
why did you decide to
to leave the most stressful job in uh in
tech sales?
I feel like the VP of sales job like I
don't know if it's like the hardest
maybe from a skill standpoint. Yep. Um
just cuz you know, cold calling and like
an SDR like that could you could make an
argument that that is hard, but the VP
job is just like dude, it's you're
managing up and down and you're just
it's like a really stressful job. Like
why did you decide to leave that
and like start your own company and like
do all of that kind of stuff. What was
that like for you?
>> So the time I decided to leave that uh a
lot of the companies were overvalued.
>> Mhm.
>> Pretty much every company was
overvalued. So for me to go get another
job as the head of sales,
big part of that compensation is uh
equity.
>> Equity, yeah.
>> So the equity wouldn't be amount
wouldn't amount to anything, right? So
um I said, "Okay, let me wait to see how
this pans out for the year. I'll do some
coaching for the year." So I started
helping some of the fastest-growing tech
companies coach their reps. Um but it
was still tough to coach at scale.
>> Yeah.
>> To our to our discussion before, you
coach the first-line leaders, you coach
the reps, and even though you go every
week, you know, they have new people
coming in, and it's just it was really
tough, but I think we did a good job.
And then initially the idea was to
create like with Simon an avatar that
would run the training programs for us.
>> Yeah.
>> Right? Uh so
>> Like it would hop on a call and run a
training program.
>> That was the idea. So then we we had I
had the uh avatar
a prototype join a an investor call, and
the investor asked me a technical
question, and I had my co-founder on the
call with me. So I was like, uh "Simon,
how do we do that?" Simon gave a great
answer. Was completely wrong, but I was
like, "Oh, this is a great How many
times in a sales cycle does a sales rep
not know an answer to a technical
question?" Say, "Oh, let me get back to
you. Let me get back to you." So we kind
of pivoted and just went more for the
subject matter expert.
>> Yeah.
>> But that still leads into So that's
getting the salesperson able to answer
questions earlier in the sales process
until a real sales engineer could get
get into the deal.
>> Yeah.
>> Um but now sales leaders are saying,
"Hey, by the way, can you also add some
coaching tips
in your engine?" So instead of just
technical answers, we're giving
suggestions in real time behind the
scenes. So if you were to
give me an objection,
we can overcome that objection, or we
can overcome that objection with that
style that that VP wants that sales rep
to do. So, it's almost coaching
literally in real time within a matter
of
1.5 seconds. Yeah. So, now that rep can
see that either
repeat that, or
maybe gives them an idea of what to say
or what path to go down. Maybe they're
not staying in the in the problem long
enough, and just based on historical
calls, you know that you should be
asking at least three, four more
questions in this problem area. And
they're jumping to another problem. So,
Simon's reminding them, "Hey, don't
forget to ask about X, Y, and Z the next
time you get a chance."
>> Yeah. So, let's talk about I'm I'm
curious and we talked about this a
little bit
during our prep call.
I'm just thinking about the objections
that you get. Like when you're selling
this, cuz
one might think like, "Well, hey, we
want something like in a Zoom call with
a rep
like telling them live, no different
than like a manager that's writing
shotgun, that's like slacking someone."
>> Yep.
>> I think a common argument might be,
"Well, isn't that really distracting? Is
that really going to help? Our reps
going to like that? You know, something,
you know,
bugging them essentially to do this
thing or handle this thing in this way."
>> Yep. I mean, that's a good that's a good
argument. And some reps may some reps
may never look at it, right? But
just to the to the point that we were
making before, there are other reps that
want to get better.
>> Yeah.
>> And they say, "Hey, if there's something
that I may be missing in real time, it's
no different than getting a Slack from
your VP when you're on the call anyway.
I don't I have a choice to either
read it, follow it, pretend I didn't see
it, continue to do my own thing." So,
it's it's very similar.
>> Yeah.
>> And I can tell you based on me, we
trained our Simon on Simon. So, our CTO
doesn't have to come on many calls at
all. And we've been doing a phenomenal
job. We only have two SDRs. Last week
alone, we set 17 new meetings. Doing
over 40 a month over the past 2 months.
So, I've been going crazy. Like on on
these calls non-stop.
>> Yeah.
>> But, I feel naked now when I don't have
Simon on running in the background.
And I remind myself, "Oh [ __ ] I don't
have Simon on. Let me turn Simon on."
Just in case there's a question that
comes up. Not so much from the sales
aspect, but um you know, and I look at
it. There are There are times There are
moments where I'm looking at it. I'm not
talking all the time like I am now. So,
there are There are times where I could
see that and I'm like, "Oh, good point.
I have to ask about that."
>> Yeah. It seems like there's a really
good and I I actually didn't understand
this part about Simon around the
technical piece
because if you think about, you know, a
lot of cybersecurity, a lot of cloud
solutions, we have a couple specific
clients I'm thinking of
where they bring a
subject matter expert onto that first
meeting to help with those questions.
And
it's extremely expensive for an
organization to be able to use
their subject matter experts in that way
in a deal that isn't even qualified yet.
>> 100%.
>> You know? So, to be able to do that and
and not have to punt every question cuz
at some point you can only punt so many
times before someone's like, "I'm not
going to take another call with this
guy."
>> And you lose credibility. And then you
have your competitor that can answer 80%
of those questions that you couldn't.
>> Yeah.
>> They're going to continue the
conversation with your competitor. And
you don't realize how many deals are are
lost on that first call or very early
stage. So, I was previously senior VP of
sales at SecurityScorecard. I helped
take them from 22 million to 100 million
in ARR within 3 years. I thought that
was pretty good. The CEO wants to grow
even faster. So, we're looking for ways
to do that. And a bottleneck that we saw
is we only had
our AE to SE ratio was 4:1. So, we had
one SE to every AE. And you can't pull
them into a first meeting. You can't
pull them But, reps still want to do
that because, hey, we're meeting with
we're meeting with B of A. It's a big
logo, big deal. Can you please come on?

and maybe answer one question and then
say afterwards that was a waste of my
time.
>> Yep.
>> Right. So, but there are moments where

fill in that gap

>> so you can keep the momentum going until

>> And Simon learns from your best reps.

>> Well, the AI coaching space right now

>> Yeah.

>> Yeah.

if your experience is different as a VP.

>> Yep.
>> Like role playing, working with a coach,
etc. The overwhelming majority of people

practice.
>> Do that. Yeah.
>> At least aren't.
>> They're not. You have to force them.
>> Yeah.
>> So maybe there's some some leadership,

>> Like actually using it and feeling

>> Yeah.
>> With me, someone asked me a question

>> It just comes out of my mouth because

>> Yeah.
>> It's like how I used to learn where I

again. I just take from them.
>> Yeah.
So it almost seems like also there's
this in the moment coaching.

>> That's how Simon knows, 100%. So by your
previous calls, your current calls, we
can tell which ones you're successful.
You identify the top reps, top
uh sales engineers.
And it's all the same data lake. We call
it vibe cloning. So, it doesn't have to
be an SAISC, it doesn't have to be an AI
sales coach, it could be an AI
uh internal trainer. Where now, as a new

being assigned Simon
that knows all the tough questions
you're going to get on a first call.

>> Knows the [clears throat] best

>> Yeah.

>> Yeah.
Yeah. I mean, it's coming from proven

doing well on calls. This type of answer
leads to a next step. This type of
answer has led to a closed one deal.
>> Yeah. So, talk a little bit more about
the AI maturity model and
like I think this is good commentary on
like what the adoption of these types of
solutions will be like.
>> Yeah, I think um you know, everyone
starts getting FOMO, you start seeing on
LinkedIn and all over the place like all
every company piloting AI and you have
no idea how far you're falling behind
until you start talking to 40 people a
month, 40 executives a month, and then
you realize everyone's behind. Right?
Everyone's talking about doing stuff,
they're maybe piloting a few things,
they're not implementing it. Um so, what
we started to do, we started to hear the
same things over and over and over again
around how companies are using AI within
their go-to-market motions. So, we
created a uh GTM AI uh maturity model,
and a lot of companies fall within the
first two levels. So, they really,
really use AI in a couple ways. One,
if they're recording a call, let's say
with Gong, they have uh prep
prep call
the moment. So
what we're doing is we're getting
companies uh to that next level where
they feel comfortable having an AI
assistant with them, either live on an
internal call, live on an external call,
but now they're getting real live
information where they can use it within
a matter of seconds. Whether we're
accounts in the moment.
>> Yeah.
It's real-time.
>> Yeah.
>> So everything else
basically in that first phase requires
some sort of like
it's it's I'm either doing a a a prep or
a postmortem and it requires me to
really intervene with it quite a bit.
Like if I'm thinking about
you know, last year for example, I can't
tell you how many companies wanted us to
train their reps on the chat GPT or
Claude or or whatever, like help us use
these tools for account research and to
write some messages and do all this
other stuff and
and I'll be honest, I was like, "Yeah,
they your reps need to know how to do
this, but I found the adoption to be
quite low."
>> Yep.
Yep.
>> Because
uh dude, there's nothing worse than I
went to

walk around and look at how people use
their computer, it's frightening.
The majority of people
>> are so slow on their computer.

tabs in LinkedIn Sales Nav. They don't

side by side.
>> Yep.
>> Um the click pa-
>> It's like
>> holy [ __ ] Like if I was creating a a

This feels a little bit Big Brother, but

most common activities.
>> Because
if someone's taking 5 minutes and 20
clicks to do something that should be
two or three clicks, like you
extrapolate that across your entire org,
across their entire day, and it's like
I'm convinced that people just need to
be better and quicker on the computer.
>> Yeah.
>> Um
>> Or or remove that completely and
automate it with AI.
>> Yes. Yes. Um my point though is just
like that phase one
in a roundabout way, my point was that
even though we have AI to do account
research in like two or three minutes
compared to the two hours it would take
you to do manually,
there isn't really that much time
savings when you take into consideration
that probably 80% of the people that we
taught couldn't replicate the prompting
and things that they were required were
required to do.
>> Yeah.
>> And I think this next level, to your
point, is stuff that's real time or
stuff that just happens under the hood
without the rep having to intervene.
>> So now they could just focus on their
their job.
>> 100%. [snorts]
So let's talk about that a bit. Like
where do you see all of this AI stuff?
Like what is the go-to market tech stack
and AI look like in in two or three
years, you think?
>> That's a good question. I do like um
I could without giving away too much of
our sales process after a successful
first meeting, what we do
for a second meeting, we say, "Okay,
what we're going to do is we're going to
go to your public knowledge base. We'll
have Simon pull down information, and
then on the next call, you ask me ques-
tech- the most technical questions you
possibly can about your product."
>> That's That's a show them moment right
there, dude. I love that.
>> It's great. So, then um
in between the meetings, we would have
our SDRs go to the public knowledge
base, download it, and then we'd train
Simon.
>> Yeah.
>> Now, we automated, we're AI native
first, and
I mean, we're trying to find every way
to be AI native. So, we're
anytime um
our AI agency deep dive on the calendar,
they go through the transcripts, they
see if there was any specific product
mentioned to understand which product to
pull down. And then, if there's no
product, it selects a product, pulls
down that information, downloads that
file, um creates an email draft to
everyone on the call saying, "Hey, this
is what Simon's been trained on." We
train Simon on that information. So, now
our SDRs don't have to do that. They can
just focus on
calling.
>> Yeah.
>> Because that would No, that would take a
decent amount of time to go manually
find what product to pull down and and
learn from.
>> Yeah.
>> But, they're going to They're going to
be so many different ways to to to do
that, and that's that's the beauty of
it. How creative can you get without
distracting people?
>> Yeah.
What are your thoughts on
cuz there's been that Salesforce stat
for the longest time that reps only
spend 25% of their time on like selling
sales activities. I think it's somewhere
between 25 and 30%.
And it just has not budged. Like every
year they do the analysis, it has not
budged.
I'm sort of of the theory that
let's say that we did take two hours of
busy work off every rep's day, and they
got 10 hours back. I don't think that
they would spend all 10 of those hours
selling. I just don't think that
Like there are other
There are other
like predictions, I guess I'll use that
word, that I've heard where
Hey, the future of the sales org is it's
going to be a third or a quarter of the
size, and like we're going to have reps
on 30 hours of calls, you know, per I
just
I don't think that the
I don't think that people are going to
actually replace the time with sales
activities. Nor do I think it's
sustainable for most people to be on
that many calls per week. Like What are
your thoughts on all of the

there to increase the efficiency in
terms of like

effective with that effort. Is it that
it's giving time back? Like What do you
think Where do you think all of this is
going, dude?
>> That's a great perspective, because I
feel like um
you don't have to All right, so you get

>> So shout out to Orum. Orum we used at
one of my other companies.
>> Mhm.
>> And then uh I I loved it so much that I
had the team use it at another company
and then
we're using it here. So with the million
things that I'm doing, there's no excuse
for me especially in the very beginning
to jump on Orum. I could spend a half
hour or an hour and it's just parallel
dialing
>> Yeah. the list, and I know I'm going to
make connects. Before that was my
biggest issue. I wasn't able to get a
connect. Maybe one connect a week, one
connect a day.
>> Yeah.
>> Um but it's such great feedback. You
have instant feedback loop on how good
your When did I get hung up on? So
early? All right, I'm rusty on my
opening. When did I get hung up on? In
the middle? There's something going on
in the middle that's not good. But then
you got to a point where I I started
getting butterflies, too. I'm like,
"Dude, I haven't cold called in a
while."
>> Yeah.
>> But to what you said earlier on, they
could tell by my voice I'm just not
someone that that just started. They
tell me something, I say something back,
and I don't mind staying on the phone
for a little bit and just having some
banter and going back and forth. Um but
then after a while I got my pitch down.
I started to set meetings. And then that
recording, I gave it to my lead SDR, and
then he started basically mimicking that
and then making fine-tuning it, making
it his own. We started laughing. I'm
like, "Dude, you're saying that you're
the
>> co-founder of the company." And I'm
like, "This is great though. You sound
amazing."
>> But um yeah, and then from there you
just like every week it gets a little
bit better, a little bit better. And
then now we know as soon as we get
someone on the phone more than likely
they're going to we're going to set up a
meeting.
>> Yeah.
>> Uh but we weren't going after the right
people at first.
And we had to hone that in and go after
the right people. So, took a little bit
of tweaking.
>> Yeah, and walk us through that journey
as much as you're willing to share
around targeting.
Like what was that journey like, and did
you make any mistakes, and all of that
kind of stuff?
>> Yeah, so in the beginning we set a ton
of meetings that with um

series A. There's still we still have
some customers there, but we realized
they fly through the sales cycle.

do. Um
for whatever reason just tough to pull
the trigger.

>> So, we're like, "Maybe we're priced too
high. Lower our price."
>> What was the size of their sales teams?
>> And that was part of it, too.
>> Mhm.
>> Sometimes two, four, right? It doesn't
really make sense.
>> Yeah.
>> Our mindset was, yeah, but you have the
founder jumping on all these calls.

tight. Even series A, you would think
you raised

You know, it's it's not going to be a
big deal.
>> Yep.
>> But then you realize, okay, let's let's
go to the people who really feel this
problem a little bit more and talk more
about the problem, not so much and I
fell into the trap.

the trap of like jumping to the demo so
so
>> Oh, yeah, you're probably excited to
show it to people.
>> I was doing the things that I hated
before as a sales leader. I

like, what am I doing?
>> Yeah.
>> Like I I I forgot about our process.
>> Yeah.
>> Right? So, um we just had to focus on
their pain, their problem. Is this
problem big enough? If we were to solve
this problem, like who else would need
to be involved? Started asking better
decision process questions.
>> Yeah.
>> Um that changed that changed a lot
because you would think like the higher

problem.
>> Yeah.

really

phone only through Orum? Dude, phone
only. I love that.

you heard of Clarify?
>> No, I haven't.
>> So, uh it's a good CRM that um
yeah, it's got AI incorporated into it.

>> Yeah, drinking your own champagne there.

>> I like them being on the call, too. So,

Like the calls that they set up.
>> So, one last thing I wanted to ask you
is just sort of unorthodox tactics that
have worked for you.
One of them you shared
hey, when BDR number one
calls a prospect and they get hung up on
or whatever or the person says not
interested, you just have the second BDR
call them a day later a couple days
later and they book a meeting. Like
that's like a really
it's like such a simple straightforward
thing that makes a lot of sense if you
think about it that I don't think is
being done at scale at most
organizations. Is there Is there How did
you land on that idea, I guess? And And
is there anything else like that that
you're finding success with that is just
like a very simple
>> So, that's the one that stuck out. I
wish I could say it's my idea. Shout out
to Sufi because I was on Orum making
calls cold calls
and someone hung up on me and I was
pissed and then I looked and it said,
"Do not call. Like don't call me again.
I'm not interested." So, then I talked
to Sufi. I'm like, "Dude,
why are why are why are why am I calling
people that are not interested?" He
said, "Oh, we do this all the time." And
then I'll have so-and-so reach out and
she books a meeting. And it works.
Like they forget they said no to
one person, someone else calls, catches
them on a better day, maybe they're more
open to understand. Like we're not We're
calling cuz we really believe we could
help them. We've done research on their
company, on their ICP, and we know we
could help them. So if they're just open
enough to listen to the problem
statement, and they give us a good
reason why they can't meet, that's fine.
But just hanging up at first, that's not
a good reason to take them off the list.
>> Yeah. And also it's
Hey, if you had the brand recognition,
which we you soon will have, I assume,
of Zoom or Shopify, it's like, yeah,
people might be like, "Dude, you guys
have called me like dozens of times."
It's like no one remembers Outbound
Squad when I call them. You know, if
it's maybe someone that is already
following me on LinkedIn, that's like
that's a completely different call
though. You know, that's not really a
cold call.
Um so the thinking there I really love.

simple and unorthodox like that that you
guys have found success with or is it
just

>> make sure not to share it with you.
